intro:
Throughout Scripture clothing stands for far more than fabric: a person's garments display their status, identity, and standing before God. To be stripped or left naked is shame and judgment; to be clothed — especially in clean, glorious robes — is honor, acceptance, and righteousness. The robe of righteousness is the Bible's image for a right standing with God that is given, not woven by the wearer.

The motif begins at the fall. Aware of their nakedness, Adam and Eve sew fig leaves, but God himself makes "garments of skin" to clothe them (Genesis 3:21) — a covering that costs the life of an animal, the first hint that sin must be covered at the price of blood. Under the law, the priests are given holy garments "for glory and for beauty" (Exodus 28:2), and the prophet Zechariah sees the meaning made plain: the high priest Joshua stands in filthy garments until the LORD commands, "Remove the filthy garments from him… I will clothe you with pure vestments" (Zechariah 3:3–5). Isaiah turns it into song: "he has clothed me with the garments of salvation; he has covered me with the robe of righteousness" (Isaiah 61:10) — even as he confesses that our own righteous deeds are but a "polluted garment" (Isaiah 64:6).

This longing is answered in Christ, who is the substance the shadows foretold. Where Adam was clothed at the cost of another's death, believers are clothed at the cost of Christ's. Paul declares that all who were baptized into Christ "have put on Christ" (Galatians 3:27), no longer having a righteousness of their own but "that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God" (Philippians 3:9). The believer is to "put on the new self, created after the likeness of God in true righteousness" (Ephesians 4:24). Jesus pictures the same exchange when the returning prodigal is dressed in "the best robe" (Luke 15:22), and warns in the wedding parable that no one may enter the feast without the wedding garment (Matthew 22:11–12).

Revelation gathers the image into its final glory. The great multitude wear white robes "washed… and made white in the blood of the Lamb" (Revelation 7:14); the Bride is granted "fine linen, bright and pure," which is "the righteous deeds of the saints" (Revelation 19:8). The motif thus traces the whole gospel of justification: the God who first covered our shame at the cost of blood clothes his people, in Christ, with a righteousness not their own — turning filthy rags into the spotless robe of the redeemed.
